lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Have patience, madam: there’s no doubt his majesty

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Will soon recover his accustom’d health.

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: In that you brook it ill, it makes him worse:

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: Therefore, for God’s sake, entertain good comfort,

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: And cheer his grace with quick and merry words.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: If he were dead, what would betide of me?

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: No other harm but loss of such a lord.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The loss of such a lord includes all harm.

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: The heavens have bless’d you with a goodly son,

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: To be your comforter when he is gone.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Oh, he is young, and his minority

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Is put unto the trust of Richard Gloucester,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: A man that loves not me, nor none of you.

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Is it concluded that he shall be protector?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: It is determined, not concluded yet:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: But so it must be, if the king miscarry.

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: Here come the lords of Buckingham and Derby.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Good time of day unto your royal grace!

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: God make your majesty joyful as you have been!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The Countess Richmond, good my Lord of Derby,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: To your good prayers will scarcely say amen.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Yet, Derby, notwithstanding she’s your wife,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And loves not me, be you, good lord, assured

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: I hate not you for her proud arrogance.

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: I do beseech you, either not believe

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: The envious slanders of her false accusers;

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Or, if she be accused in true report,

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Bear with her weakness, which, I think, proceeds

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: From wayward sickness, and no grounded malice.

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Saw you the king to-day, my Lord of Derby?

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: But now the Duke of Buckingham and I

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Are come from visiting his majesty.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: What likelihood of his amendment, lords?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Madam, good hope; his grace speaks cheerfully.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: God grant him health! Did you confer with him?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Madam, we did: he desires to make atonement

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Betwixt the Duke of Gloucester and your brothers,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And betwixt them and my lord chamberlain;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And sent to warn them to his royal presence.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Would all were well! but that will never be:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: I fear our happiness is at the highest.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: They do me wrong, and I will not endure it:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Who are they that complain unto the king,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That I, forsooth, am stern and love them not?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: By holy Paul, they love his grace but lightly

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That fill his ears with such dissentious rumours.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Because I cannot flatter and speak fair,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Smile in men’s faces, smooth, deceive and cog,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Duck with French nods and apish courtesy,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I must be held a rancorous enemy.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Cannot a plain man live and think no harm,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But thus his simple truth must be abused

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: By silken, sly, insinuating Jacks?

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: To whom in all this presence speaks your grace?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To thee, that hast nor honesty nor grace.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: When have I injured thee? when done thee wrong?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Or thee? or thee? or any of your faction?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: A plague upon you all! His royal person—

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Whom God preserve better than you would wish!—

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Cannot be quiet scarce a breathing-while,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But you must trouble him with lewd complaints.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Brother of Gloucester, you mistake the matter.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The king, of his own royal disposition,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And not provoked by any suitor else;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Aiming, belike, at your interior hatred,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Which in your outward actions shows itself

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Against my kindred, brothers, and myself,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Makes him to send; that thereby he may gather

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The ground of your ill-will, and so remove it.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I cannot tell: the world is grown so bad,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That wrens make prey where eagles dare not perch:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Since every Jack became a gentleman,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: There’s many a gentle person made a Jack.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Come, come, we know your meaning, brother Gloucester;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: You envy my advancement and my friends’:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: God grant we never may have need of you!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Meantime, God grants that we have need of you:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Your brother is imprison’d by your means,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Myself disgraced, and the nobility

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Held in contempt; whilst many fair promotions

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Are daily given to ennoble those

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That scarce, some two days since, were worth a noble.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: By Him that raised me to this careful height

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: From that contented hap which I enjoy’d,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: I never did incense his majesty

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Against the Duke of Clarence, but have been

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: An earnest advocate to plead for him.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: My lord, you do me shameful injury,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Falsely to draw me in these vile suspects.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: You may deny that you were not the cause

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Of my Lord Hastings’ late imprisonment.

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: She may, my lord, for—

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: She may, Lord Rivers! why, who knows not so?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: She may do more, sir, than denying that:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: She may help you to many fair preferments,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And then deny her aiding hand therein,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And lay those honours on your high deserts.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What may she not? She may, yea, marry, may she—

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: What, marry, may she?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What, marry, may she! marry with a king,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: A bachelor, a handsome stripling too:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I wis your grandam had a worser match.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: My Lord of Gloucester, I have too long borne

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Your blunt upbraidings and your bitter scoffs:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: By heaven, I will acquaint his majesty

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: With those gross taunts I often have endured.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: I had rather be a country servant-maid

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Than a great queen, with this condition,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: To be thus taunted, scorn’d, and baited at:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And lessen’d be that small, God, I beseech thee!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thy honour, state and seat is due to me.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What! threat you me with telling of the king?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Tell him, and spare not: look, what I have said

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I will avouch in presence of the king:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I dare adventure to be sent to the Tower.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: ’Tis time to speak; my pains are quite forgot.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Out, devil! I remember them too well:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thou slewest my husband Henry in the Tower,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And Edward, my poor son, at Tewksbury.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Ere you were queen, yea, or your husband king,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I was a pack-horse in his great affairs;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: A weeder-out of his proud adversaries,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: A liberal rewarder of his friends:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To royalise his blood I spilt mine own.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Yea, and much better blood than his or thine.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: In all which time you and your husband Grey

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Were factious for the house of Lancaster;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And, Rivers, so were you. Was not your husband

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: In Margaret’s battle at Saint Alban’s slain?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Let me put in your minds, if you forget,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What you have been ere now, and what you are;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Withal, what I have been, and what I am.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: A murderous villain, and so still thou art.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Poor Clarence did forsake his father, Warwick;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Yea, and forswore himself—which Jesu pardon!—

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Which God revenge!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To fight on Edward’s party for the crown;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And for his meed, poor lord, he is mew’d up.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I would to God my heart were flint, like Edward’s;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Or Edward’s soft and pitiful, like mine:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I am too childish-foolish for this world.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Hie thee to hell for shame, and leave the world,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thou cacodemon! there thy kingdom is.

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: My Lord of Gloucester, in those busy days

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Which here you urge to prove us enemies,

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: We follow’d then our lord, our lawful king:

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: So should we you, if you should be our king.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: If I should be! I had rather be a pedlar:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Far be it from my heart, the thought of it!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: As little joy, my lord, as you suppose

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: You should enjoy, were you this country’s king,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: As little joy may you suppose in me,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: That I enjoy, being the queen thereof.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: A little joy enjoys the queen thereof;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: For I am she, and altogether joyless.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: I can no longer hold me patient. Advancing.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Hear me, you wrangling pirates, that fall out

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: In sharing that which you have pill’d from me!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Which of you trembles not that looks on me?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: If not, that, I being queen, you bow like subjects,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Yet that, by you deposed, you quake like rebels?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: O gentle villain, do not turn away!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Foul wrinkled witch, what makest thou in my sight?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: But repetition of what thou hast marr’d;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: That will I make before I let thee go.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Wert thou not banished on pain of death?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: I was; but I do find more pain in banishment

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Than death can yield me here by my abode.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: A husband and a son thou owest to me;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And thou a kingdom; all of you allegiance:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: The sorrow that I have, by right is yours,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And all the pleasures you usurp are mine.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The curse my noble father laid on thee,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: When thou didst crown his warlike brows with paper

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And with thy scorns drew’st rivers from his eyes,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And then, to dry them, gavest the duke a clout

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Steep’d in the faultless blood of pretty Rutland—

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: His curses, then from bitterness of soul

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Denounced against thee, are all fall’n upon thee;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And God, not we, hath plagued thy bloody deed.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: So just is God, to right the innocent.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: O, ’twas the foulest deed to slay that babe,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: And the most merciless that e’er was heard of!

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Tyrants themselves wept when it was reported.

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: No man but prophesied revenge for it.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Northumberland, then present, wept to see it.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: What! were you snarling all before I came,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Ready to catch each other by the throat,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And turn you all your hatred now on me?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Did York’s dread curse prevail so much with heaven

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: That Henry’s death, my lovely Edward’s death,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Their kingdom’s loss, my woeful banishment,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Could all but answer for that peevish brat?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Can curses pierce the clouds and enter heaven?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Why, then, give way, dull clouds, to my quick curses!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: If not by war, by surfeit die your king,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: As ours by murder, to make him a king!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Edward thy son, which now is Prince of Wales,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: For Edward my son, which was Prince of Wales,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Die in his youth by like untimely violence!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thyself a queen, for me that was a queen,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Outlive thy glory, like my wretched self!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Long mayst thou live to wail thy children’s loss;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And see another, as I see thee now,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Deck’d in thy rights, as thou art stall’d in mine!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Long die thy happy days before thy death;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And, after many lengthen’d hours of grief,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Die neither mother, wife, nor England’s queen!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Rivers and Dorset, you were standers by,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And so wast thou, Lord Hastings, when my son

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Was stabb’d with bloody daggers: God, I pray him,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: That none of you may live your natural age,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: But by some unlook’d accident cut off!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Have done thy charm, thou hateful wither’d hag!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And leave out thee? stay, dog, for thou shalt hear me.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: If heaven have any grievous plague in store

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Exceeding those that I can wish upon thee,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: O, let them keep it till thy sins be ripe,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And then hurl down their indignation

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: On thee, the troubler of the poor world’s peace!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: The worm of conscience still begnaw thy soul!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thy friends suspect for traitors while thou livest,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And take deep traitors for thy dearest friends!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: No sleep close up that deadly eye of thine,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Unless it be whilst some tormenting dream

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Affrights thee with a hell of ugly devils!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thou elvish-mark’d, abortive, rooting hog!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thou that wast seal’d in thy nativity

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: The slave of nature and the son of hell!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thou slander of thy mother’s heavy womb!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thou loathed issue of thy father’s loins!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thou rag of honour! thou detested—

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Margaret.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Richard!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Ha!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: I call thee not.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I cry thee mercy then, for I had thought

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That thou hadst call’d me all these bitter names.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Why, so I did; but look’d for no reply.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: O, let me make the period to my curse!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: ’Tis done by me, and ends in “Margaret.”

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Thus have you breathed your curse against yourself.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Poor painted queen, vain flourish of my fortune!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Why strew’st thou sugar on that bottled spider,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Whose deadly web ensnareth thee about?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Fool, fool! thou whet’st a knife to kill thyself.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: The time will come when thou shalt wish for me

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: To help thee curse that poisonous bunch-back’d toad.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: False-boding woman, end thy frantic curse,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Lest to thy harm thou move our patience.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Foul shame upon you! you have all moved mine.

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Were you well served, you would be taught your duty.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: To serve me well, you all should do me duty,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Teach me to be your queen, and you my subjects:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: O, serve me well, and teach yourselves that duty!

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: Dispute not with her; she is lunatic.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Peace, master marquess, you are malapert:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Your fire-new stamp of honour is scarce current.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: O, that your young nobility could judge

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: What ’twere to lose it, and be miserable!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: They that stand high have many blasts to shake them;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And if they fall, they dash themselves to pieces.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Good counsel, marry: learn it, learn it, marquess.

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: It toucheth you, my lord, as much as me.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Yea, and much more: but I was born so high,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Our aery buildeth in the cedar’s top,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And dallies with the wind and scorns the sun.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And turns the sun to shade; alas! alas!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Witness my son, now in the shade of death;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Whose bright out-shining beams thy cloudy wrath

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Hath in eternal darkness folded up.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Your aery buildeth in our aery’s nest.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: O God, that seest it, do not suffer it;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: As it was won with blood, lost be it so!

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Have done! for shame, if not for charity.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Urge neither charity nor shame to me:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Uncharitably with me have you dealt,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And shamefully by you my hopes are butcher’d.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: My charity is outrage, life my shame;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And in that shame still live my sorrow’s rage!

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Have done, have done.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: O princely Buckingham, I’ll kiss thy hand,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: In sign of league and amity with thee:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Now fair befall thee and thy noble house!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thy garments are not spotted with our blood,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Nor thou within the compass of my curse.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Nor no one here; for curses never pass

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: The lips of those that breathe them in the air.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: I’ll not believe but they ascend the sky,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And there awake God’s gentle-sleeping peace.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: O Buckingham, take heed of yonder dog!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Look, when he fawns, he bites; and when he bites,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: His venom tooth will rankle to the death:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Have not to do with him, beware of him;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Sin, death, and hell have set their marks on him,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And all their ministers attend on him.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What doth she say, my Lord of Buckingham?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Nothing that I respect, my gracious lord.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: What, dost thou scorn me for my gentle counsel?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And soothe the devil that I warn thee from?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: O, but remember this another day,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: When he shall split thy very heart with sorrow,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And say poor Margaret was a prophetess!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Live each of you the subjects to his hate,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And he to yours, and all of you to God’s!

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: My hair doth stand on end to hear her curses.

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: And so doth mine: I muse why she’s at liberty.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I cannot blame her: by God’s holy mother,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: She hath had too much wrong; and I repent

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My part thereof that I have done to her.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: I never did her any, to my knowledge.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But you have all the vantage of her wrong.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I was too hot to do somebody good,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That is too cold in thinking of it now.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Marry, as for Clarence, he is well repaid;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He is frank’d up to fatting for his pains:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: God pardon them that are the cause of it!

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: A virtuous and a Christian-like conclusion,

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: To pray for them that have done scathe to us.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: So do I ever: aside being well advised.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: For had I cursed now, I had cursed myself.

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: Madam, his majesty doth call for you;

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: And for your grace; and you, my noble lords.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Catesby, we come. Lords, will you go with us?

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Madam, we will attend your grace.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I do the wrong, and first begin to brawl.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The secret mischiefs that I set abroach

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I lay unto the grievous charge of others.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Clarence, whom I, indeed, have laid in darkness,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I do beweep to many simple gulls;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Namely, to Hastings, Derby, Buckingham;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And say it is the queen and her allies

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That stir the king against the duke my brother.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Now, they believe it; and withal whet me

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To be revenged on Rivers, Vaughan, Grey:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But then I sigh; and, with a piece of scripture,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Tell them that God bids us do good for evil:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And thus I clothe my naked villany

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: With old odd ends stolen out of holy writ;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And seem a saint, when most I play the devil.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: We are, my lord; and come to have the warrant,

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: That we may be admitted where he is.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Well thought upon; I have it here about me. Gives the warrant.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: When you have done, repair to Crosby Place.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But, sirs, be sudden in the execution,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Withal obdurate, do not hear him plead;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: For Clarence is well-spoken, and perhaps

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: May move your hearts to pity, if you mark him.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Tush!

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Fear not, my lord, we will not stand to prate;

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Talkers are no good doers: be assured

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: We come to use our hands and not our tongues.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Your eyes drop millstones, when fools’ eyes drop tears:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I like you, lads; about your business straight;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Go, go, dispatch.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: We will, my noble lord.

stage_directions.text: Enter Queen Elizabeth, Lord Rivers, and Lord Grey.
Small joy have I in being England’s queen.
But, soft! here come my executioners. How now, my hardy, stout resolved mates! Are you now going to dispatch this deed?

glosses.definition: happen to me; become of me
the period before a king comes of age; here, Prince Edward's
Richard Duke of Gloucester — to be Lord Protector during the young prince's minority
Lord Protector of the Realm — the regent who governs for an underage king
Lord Stanley, Earl of Derby — later Richmond's ally; historical figure crucial at Bosworth
Margaret Beaufort, mother of Henry Tudor (Richmond) — Derby's second wife; the single most politically active woman of the period
our fortune has reached its peak (and will now decline) — Elizabeth voices the tragic arc
Richard's habitual oath; cf. 1.1.137
cheat, deceive (esp. in dice)
affected foreign-style bows and imitative etiquette — Richard's sneer at court manners
smooth, devious, sneaking fellows — 'Jack' = contemptible low fellow

glosses.phrase: wrens make prey where eagles dare not perch
glosses.definition: small/base creatures seize what the great fear to touch — the inverted hierarchy complaint
commoner became a gentleman — Richard's complaint about upstart Woodvilles
worth one noble (gold coin, ~6 shillings 8 pence) — Richard mocks the Woodvilles as recently worthless
I know your grandmother — 'wis' = know (archaic); a sneer at Elizabeth's lineage
begone, fiend — Margaret's first direct address to Richard
Edward of Lancaster, killed at Tewkesbury — Margaret's murdered son
workhorse — Gloucester casts himself as Edward's labour-beast
two battles of St Albans (1455, 1461) in the Wars of the Roses
evil demon; literally 'bad spirit' (Greek). Margaret's theological-demonological vocabulary
stage direction — Margaret steps forward from concealment to confront the court
quarreling thieves — those fighting over stolen goods
plundered, pillaged
removed from the throne — Margaret was queen of England 1445–1461, deposed at the Yorkist triumph
Richard's insult pattern throughout; Margaret as cursed hag-figure
stay, remaining here

glosses.phrase: The curse my noble father laid on thee
glosses.definition: Richard recalls York's death-curse at Wakefield — recounted in 3 Henry VI 1.4

glosses.phrase: crown his warlike brows with paper
glosses.definition: the paper crown Margaret used to mock York before his execution at Wakefield
Edmund, Earl of Rutland — York's young son, killed by Clifford at Wakefield
the Percy earl present at Rutland's death, historically reported as weeping
Margaret's forced exile after Tewkesbury
reach heaven — whether prayers can ascend was a live devotional question
over-indulgence — Margaret curses Edward IV to die of gluttony; he did

glosses.phrase: Edward my son, which was Prince of Wales
glosses.definition: Margaret's murdered son — his namesake now threatened with the same fate
the 'undying worm' (Isaiah 66:24, Mark 9:44–48) — hell's torment of unrepentant conscience
marked by elves/fairies at birth — i.e., deformed by supernatural malice
monstrously misshapen from birth
boar rooting in earth — Richard's heraldic badge was the white boar; Margaret weaponizes it
deformity was thought to signal moral slavery under a corrupt Nature

glosses.phrase: slander of thy mother’s heavy womb
glosses.definition: disgrace to the womb that bore thee

glosses.phrase: ’Tis done by me, and ends in “Margaret.”
glosses.definition: Richard's trick — he interrupts to turn the curse's last word against her
Margaret's dismissal of Elizabeth — painted = cosmetically enhanced; false finery
hunchbacked spider — 'bottled' = swollen, pot-bellied; Margaret's signature insult for Richard
venomous hunchbacked toad — combining the two staples of Elizabethan loathing-imagery
impudent, saucy
freshly minted — Dorset's marquisate is newly granted
proverbial; Margaret enunciates the Fortune-wheel commonplace

glosses.phrase: aery buildeth in the cedar’s top
glosses.definition: eagle's nest (aery) built at the top of the cedar — royal-lineage image; 'aery' = eagle's eyrie
cease, stop

glosses.phrase: curses never pass / The lips of those that breathe them
glosses.definition: Buckingham's skeptical commonplace — curses don't rise past the mouth; Margaret immediately contradicts him
the trinity of evil — theological commonplace
Margaret's claim to Cassandra-function — to be remembered later as a prophet
confined (as in a 'frank' = sty) to fatten for slaughter — pig-husbandry image for Clarence
stage direction — the aside clarifies Richard's hypocrisy for the audience
set flowing, broached (as a cask) — Richard's mischiefs released
easy dupes; fools easily tricked
the three Woodville-aligned men Richard will execute at Pomfret — named together, to be killed together

glosses.phrase: old odd ends stolen out of holy writ
glosses.definition: scraps pilfered from Scripture — Richard's rhetorical method explicitly named

glosses.phrase: seem a saint, when most I play the devil
glosses.definition: Richard's credo; the play's thematic motto
hardened, unyielding
tears as heavy as millstones — i.e. no tears at all (millstones don't fall); a professional-killer boast

annotations.title: Margaret of Anjou and a deliberate historical impossibility
annotations.body: The historical Margaret of Anjou, widow of Henry VI, was banished from England in 1475 and died in France in August 1482 — nine months before Edward IV's death in April 1483, which is where Richard III's action begins. She cannot be present at Edward's court. Shakespeare knows this: his own 3 Henry VI had already dramatized her post-Tewkesbury exile. He keeps her alive onstage anyway, and the decision is dramaturgically load-bearing. Margaret is not there as a Tudor-history figure but as a Greek-chorus figure — a prophetic memory outside the action, scoring each death against the roster of earlier Lancastrian deaths the Yorkists caused. Her presence insists that the Wars of the Roses are a single moral ledger, and that the debts accumulated in the earlier plays have not been paid. The anachronism is the point: by keeping Margaret alive, Shakespeare keeps Henry VI's ghost politically alive too.
sources: Patricia-Ann Lee, "Reflections of Power: Margaret of Anjou and the Dark Side of Queenship" (1986)

annotations.title: The curse as structural engine
annotations.body: Margaret's long curse sequence is not ornament. Every prediction in it is fulfilled by the play's end, and the method of each death is named in advance. Edward IV will "by surfeit die" (he does, offstage, between 1.3 and 2.1). His son the Prince of Wales, now a boy, "die in his youth by like untimely violence" (he is murdered in 4.3). Elizabeth will "outlive thy glory, like my wretched self" (she is reduced to negotiating with her children's killer in 4.4). Rivers, Grey, and Hastings each receive specific dooms and die at Pomfret (3.3) and the Tower (3.4). Buckingham — uncursed here, because he has not yet harmed Margaret — receives a delayed curse at the end of the scene ("take heed of yonder dog!") and dies at 5.1 quoting her words. The play works as a checklist: Margaret names the deaths, the plot delivers them.
sources: Wolfgang Clemen, A Commentary on Shakespeare's Richard III (1968)

annotations.title: The vocabulary of imprecation
annotations.body: Margaret's curse on Richard (lines 218–243) is the densest single passage of imprecatory vocabulary in Shakespeare. "Worm of conscience" (Isaiah 66:24), "elvish-mark'd" (marked by fairies at birth, i.e., deformed as a supernatural sign), "abortive, rooting hog" (monstrous boar — Richard's heraldic badge weaponized), "slave of nature" (ruled by a corrupt Nature), "bottled spider" (swollen-bodied, hunchbacked), "bunch-back'd toad" — the insults cluster from Scripture, folk-demonology, heraldry, and creature-imagery in rapid succession. The vocabulary is not random. It draws on a tradition of cursing-speeches in medieval romance and early Tudor drama (the "flyting" tradition), and it is also what Elizabeth will explicitly ask Margaret to teach her in 4.4 ("O thou well skill'd in curses, stay awhile, / And teach me how to curse mine enemies!"). Cursing, in this play, is a craft.
sources: Ian Frederick Moulton, "'A Monster Great Deformed'" (1996)

annotations.title: "Take heed of yonder dog!" — Buckingham's uncursed, then cursed
annotations.body: Margaret's curse ends without including Buckingham by name — he has not yet done her wrong. But in a coda (lines 277–291), she blesses him, kisses his hand, and warns him against Richard: "Take heed of yonder dog! / Look, when he fawns, he bites." Buckingham, who cannot yet imagine needing the warning, dismisses it politely. In 5.1, minutes before his execution, he will quote it back: "'When he,' quoth she, 'shall split thy heart with sorrow, / Remember Margaret was a prophetess.'" The scene's structural symmetry is exact. Buckingham's blindness to warning is the play's moral arithmetic demonstrated in miniature: you cannot take Richard's hand and expect to keep your head. Margaret's foreknowledge is the only thing in the play that keeps pace with Richard's cunning; Buckingham's willingness to ignore her is his specific tragedy.
sources: Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)
A. P. Rossiter, Angel with Horns (1961)

annotations.title: "Seem a saint, when most I play the devil"
annotations.body: Richard's soliloquy at the scene's end states the play's operative motto: "Thus I clothe my naked villany / With old odd ends stolen out of holy writ; / And seem a saint, when most I play the devil." The rhetorical self-consciousness here is what distinguishes Richard from the medieval Vice-figures he descends from. The Vice was opaque to himself; Richard is his own critic. He knows the technique, names it, and mocks his own success at it. This is both the play's great dramatic achievement — Richard's interiority visible from the inside — and its eventual ethical problem. We see him see himself clearly and choose wrongly. When the ghosts come in 5.3, they come to a conscience that has never been fooled by the performance. The soliloquy's final line is, functionally, Richard's admission that he has known all along.
sources: Bernard Spivack, Shakespeare and the Allegory of Evil (1958)
Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: The murderers arrive, and conscience becomes comic
annotations.body: The scene's hinge — Richard's soliloquy giving way to the entrance of the two First and Second Murderers — introduces the play's tonal counter-current. The killers' banter ("eyes drop millstones, when fools' eyes drop tears") and the Second Murderer's coming comic debate with his own conscience (1.4) belong to a different tonal register from the cursing of 1.3. Shakespeare is experimenting with the mixed mode that will become Hamlet's gravediggers, Macbeth's Porter, and Lear's Fool — high tragic matter surrounded by low-comic ministers. The murderers' casualness is not relief but contrast: they are hired, they have a warrant, they will not plead, and their conscience (when it speaks) is a household pest. Richard is more terrifying because his instruments are banal.
sources: Harry Levin, The Question of Hamlet (1959)

trials.prompt: Margaret of Anjou's appearance at Edward IV's court is historically impossible. Why?
options: She was still imprisoned in the Tower at the time of the play's action
She had returned to France after Tewkesbury and died in 1482, before the play's action begins
She never left France after her marriage to Henry VI
She had renounced royal life and entered a convent

trials.prompt: What function does Margaret serve dramaturgically, given her historical impossibility?
options: She is a realist character whose anachronism is an oversight
She functions as a Greek-chorus / prophetic-memory figure, keeping the earlier plays' moral ledger open
She represents an alternate-historical counterfactual
She is a comic character, providing relief from tragic events

trials.prompt: What does Margaret curse Edward IV with, specifically?
options: Death in battle
Death "by surfeit" — over-indulgence (which does kill him offstage)
Poisoning by the Woodvilles
A slow wasting disease lasting seven years

trials.prompt: Margaret's epithets for Richard — "elvish-mark'd, abortive, rooting hog," "bottled spider," "bunch-back'd toad" — draw on which overlapping traditions?
options: Classical Greek tragedy and Roman satire only
Scripture, folk-demonology, heraldry, and creature-imagery in rapid combination
Medieval courtly love poetry
Euphuism — the decorative style of John Lyly

trials.prompt: Why does Margaret not curse Buckingham in her main curse, only warning him at the scene's end?
options: Because she secretly loves him
Because he has not yet done her wrong; her curse-logic is strictly retributive
Because he is too powerful to curse safely
Because he was not present for her husband's murder

trials.prompt: Which Richard line from this scene serves as the play's operating motto for his villainy?
options: "A plague upon you all!"
"I cannot tell: the world is grown so bad"
"Seem a saint, when most I play the devil"
"I am a pack-horse in his great affairs"

trials.prompt: How does Richard distinguish himself from the medieval Vice-figures he descends from?
options: He never speaks in rhyme
He refuses to address the audience
He is his own critic — he names and mocks his own rhetorical technique
He is a purely comic character, not tragic

trials.prompt: What is the tonal effect of the Murderers' entrance at the scene's end?
options: It heightens the tragic intensity of the preceding curse
It provides contrast: the banal, comic-domestic register of Richard's hired instruments counterpoints the cursing
It breaks the fourth wall, turning the play into a comedy
It signals the end of the scene with a formal flourish
